..summary: MicrosoftAuthorizationGateFilter calls getSession(false) twice in callback branch

evidence: Lines 60-62: request.getSession(false) is called to null-check, then called again to invalidate, rather than capturing the result in a local variable
detail.suggestion: Capture session in a local variable: var session = request.getSession(false); if (session != null) session.invalidate();

..summary: UserResponse stores provider and lastLoginFlow as String instead of their enum types

evidence: Lines 15, 17: record fields 'String provider' and 'String lastLoginFlow' lose enum type safety. Line 28-30: 'user.getProvider().name()' and 'user.getLastLoginFlow().name()' explicitly convert enums to strings. Jackson serializes enums as their name() by default, so the enum types could be used directly
detail.suggestion: Change record fields to use enum types: 'AuthProvider provider, AuthFlow lastLoginFlow' and pass enum values directly: 'user.getProvider(), user.getLastLoginFlow()'. Jackson will serialize them as strings automatically

..summary: AuthCompletionService has 4 thin convenience methods that just normalize then delegate

evidence: Lines 55-57: completeGoogleAuthentication normalizes via providerIdentityNormalizer then calls completeAuthentication — two calls could be inlined at caller
Lines 61-63: completeMicrosoftAuthentication follows identical normalize+delegate pattern
Lines 67-69 and 73-75: WithCode variants repeat the same pattern with completeAuthenticationWithCode
Each wrapper has a restating Javadoc comment (lines 54, 60, 66, 72) that echoes the method name
detail.suggestion: Inline the normalize+complete calls at the 4 call sites (AuthController and OAuth2SuccessHandler) and remove the convenience methods. This eliminates the indirection layer and the restating Javadoc. The two core methods (completeAuthentication, completeAuthenticationWithCode) remain as the service's public API.

..summary: Restating Javadoc on self-documenting methods adds noise without insight

evidence: AuthController line 179: '/** Extracts the challenge session ID from the request cookie. */' on extractChallengeSessionId — name says exactly this
AuthController line 152: '/** Exchanges a single-use auth code... */' on exchangeCode endpoint — name + @PostMapping path are sufficient
AuthFlow lines 7-13: 6-line Javadoc on a 10-line fromValue parser — @param and @throws restate what valueOf + nullcheck already express
detail.suggestion: Remove Javadoc from extractChallengeSessionId (line 179) and exchangeCode (line 152) where method name is self-documenting. Trim AuthFlow.fromValue Javadoc to just the @throws tag (non-obvious behavior).

..summary: ProviderConfigResponse is not part of the sealed AuthApiResponse hierarchy despite being served from the same /auth base path

evidence: ProviderConfigController is @RequestMapping("/auth") and returns ResponseEntity<ProviderConfigResponse>, while AuthController also @RequestMapping("/auth") returns ResponseEntity<AuthApiResponse>
AuthApiResponse sealed interface permits only ErrorResponse, LogoutResponse, MicrosoftChallengeResponse, TokenResponse — ProviderConfigResponse is a standalone record
detail.suggestion: Either include ProviderConfigResponse in the AuthApiResponse sealed hierarchy, or move ProviderConfigController to a distinct base path (e.g., /config/providers) to make the API surface boundary explicit. The sealed hierarchy already captures the auth action responses well; config is arguably a different concern.

..summary: The /auth/** permitAll wildcard automatically makes any future endpoint under /auth/ publicly accessible

evidence: SecurityConfig.java:59-63 — .requestMatchers("/auth/**").permitAll() covers all current and future /auth/ endpoints
Currently all /auth endpoints are correctly public (verify, challenge, exchange, logout, providers), but the wildcard pattern means a developer adding e.g., /auth/admin would inherit public access
detail.suggestion: Consider enumerating specific public paths instead of using the wildcard, or add a comment documenting the intent that all /auth/ endpoints are designed to be public. Given the small number of endpoints, explicit listing would be safer: .requestMatchers("/auth/google/verify", "/auth/microsoft/**", "/auth/exchange", "/auth/logout", "/auth/providers").permitAll()

..summary: AuthController Google disabled response uses inline construction while Microsoft uses extracted helper

evidence: Line 76-77: Google disabled returns inline ResponseEntity.status(503).body(new ErrorResponse("Google client-side login is disabled"))
Line 106: Microsoft disabled returns microsoftClientSideDisabledResponse() via extracted private helper (line 214-216)
Both are 503 responses with provider-specific messages — same behavioral intent, different construction patterns
detail.suggestion: Extract a parameterized helper like providerClientSideDisabledResponse(String provider) or create a matching googleClientSideDisabledResponse() to align with the Microsoft pattern

..summary: Legacy migration branches create secondary lookup/write paths across UserService and RedisAuthCodeStore

evidence: UserService.syncUser() has google_id fallback on lines 51-54 creating a secondary lookup path
RedisAuthCodeStore.exchangeCode() uses dual key prefix routing (LEGACY_KEY_PREFIX vs V2_KEY_PREFIX) with a compatibility fallback that checks both
UserService.createNewUser() conditionally sets google_id for Google users (line 73), and recoverFromConcurrentCreation() has provider-specific recovery branches
detail.suggestion: These paths are well-documented with removal criteria. Track migration completion (all Google users re-authenticated post-V3) to enable cleanup. Consider adding a startup log message showing current migration mode to make the active code path explicit.

..summary: buildMicrosoftConfig has dense conditional logic with repeated null checks on microsoftRollout

evidence: Lines 70-81: microsoftRollout != null is checked 4 times across the method — once each for serverSideEnabled, clientSideEnabled, and two warning conditions. The repeated null guard pattern obscures the core logic of mapping rollout flags to config values.
detail.suggestion: Extract an early-return guard at the top of the method: if microsoftRollout is null, return a fully-disabled config immediately. This eliminates 4 redundant null checks and makes the happy path clearer.

..summary: Sibling store interfaces use different error strategies: AuthCodeStore throws on failure, MicrosoftChallengeStore returns Optional

evidence: AuthCodeStore.exchangeCode() javadoc: '@throws IllegalArgumentException if the code is invalid, expired, or already consumed'
MicrosoftChallengeStore.consumeNonce() javadoc: 'Returns empty if session is expired or challengeId doesn't match'
Both handle the same semantic operation (single-use token consumption) with different failure signaling strategies
AuthController compensates: lines 134-137 check expectedNonce.isEmpty() for challenge store vs lines 160-166 catch IllegalArgumentException for code store
detail.suggestion: Align the strategies: either both throw on invalid/expired tokens (with the controller catching), or both return Optional (with the controller checking). The Optional pattern is more appropriate for 'not found' semantics.

narrative.headline: Score plateaued at 87.3 for 5 scans. Code quality (55.6%) is where the breakthrough is. 152 wontfix items may also be worth revisiting. (25 review work items — run `desloppify show review --status open`)
